ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general udon noodle dish class; no exact stir-fried udon with vegetables entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle dish — wheat-based noodles, cooked with sauce and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated yaki udon or stir-fried udon code. A0CKP (noodle dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific prepared noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of stir-fried udon noodles with vegetables and sauce, approximately 400 g, as typically served at a Japanese-inspired restaurant or food delivery platform in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodles, japanese, udon, cooked (FDC ID 2103689) and Noodles, japanese, somen, cooked (FDC ID 169730); values adapted for composite stir-fried udon dish with sauce and vegetables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for this specific GrabFood udon dish exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo. Values are estimated by combining USDA cooked udon noodle data (FDC ID 2103689) with typical stir-fry sauce and vegetable additions (soy sauce, dashi, mirin, mushrooms, onions, bell peppers, oil). The input record's per_100g values (energy 137.5 kcal, protein 6.25 g, fat 4.5 g, carbohydrate 17.5 g) are broadly consistent with this USDA-anchored composition and are retained as the best available estimate. Provenance is ai_generated from GrabFood; confidence is medium. All values documented as estimated. USDA FoodData Central is the approved primary source used; GrabFood is not used as a nutrient source.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Udon noodles, white, boiled (GI ~55)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain boiled udon noodles (white wheat flour). Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category. The stir-fried preparation with sauce and vegetables may modestly lower the effective GI due to added fat, protein, and fibre from vegetables; however, 55 is retained as a conservative estimate for the noodle base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 70.0 g) / 100 = 38.5, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 17.5 × 4 = 70.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g; a 400 g serving delivers 1200 mg sodium, representing approximately 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and dashi seasoning, limit portion size to no more than 200 g, and pair the dish with high-potassium vegetables such as mushrooms and onions to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Udon noodles are made from refined wheat flour with a GI of approximately 55 (low boundary); a 400 g serving delivers 70 g of carbohydrate with only 5 g of dietary fibre, producing a high glycemic load of 38.5.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, substitute udon with konjac or shirataki noodles where possible, increase the proportion of non-starchy vegetables, and monitor post-meal blood glucose response given the high glycemic load per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.0 g per 100 g and trans fat is negligible at 0.03 g per 100 g; the dish is not deep-fried, and cholesterol is modest at 12.5 mg per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish presents a low cardiovascular fat risk profile due to minimal saturated and trans fat; however, the high sodium content (300 mg/100 g) warrants caution for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular disease, and portion control is advised to keep total sodium intake within daily limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g), mushroom beta-glucans with immunomodulatory properties, and antioxidant-rich vegetables; however, bell peppers are nightshades which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may generally tolerate this dish due to its low saturated fat and anti-inflammatory vegetable components, but should monitor nightshade tolerance (bell peppers) and request reduced soy sauce to keep sodium below 400 mg per 100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Udon Noodles with Stir-Fried Vegetables and Sauce is a Japanese-inspired noodle dish consisting of thick, chewy wheat-flour udon noodles that are first boiled and then pan-fried or stir-fried with a savoury sauce base of soy sauce, dashi broth, and mirin, combined with vegetables such as mushrooms, onions, and bell peppers. The dish is available on GrabFood Singapore under the listing name 'A&W', though the food content is a Japanese udon preparation rather than the A&W fast-food brand. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 137.5 kcal, 6.25 g protein, 4.5 g fat (of which 1.0 g is saturated), 17.5 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g sugar, 1.25 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The glycemic index of udon noodles is approximately 55 (low GI boundary per University of Sydney GI Database), and a standard 400 g bowl delivers a glycemic load of approximately 38.5, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content of 300 mg per 100 g (1200 mg per bowl) is a key concern for hypertensive individuals. The dish is not deep-fried and has a low saturated fat profile, making it relatively favourable for cardiovascular health when consumed in moderate portions. Clinically, it is most relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management (high GL per serving) and hypertension (high sodium per serving). The dish is not suitable for gluten-free or halal-certified diets without modification, and contains fish-derived dashi, making it unsuitable for vegetarians and vegans in its standard form.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of udon?

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific GrabFood udon dish exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA cooked udon noodle data (FDC ID 2103689) with typical stir-fry sauce and vegetable additions. The input record's values are broadly consistent with this composition and are retained as the best available estimate. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain boiled udon noodles. Confidence is medium due to the estimated nature of the composite dish values and the ambiguous food name 'A&W' in the source record.
notes: 1. The input record name 'A&W' is ambiguous — the description and ingredients clearly indicate a Japanese udon noodle dish, not the A&W fast-food brand. The canonical food name has been corrected to 'Udon Noodles with Stir-Fried Vegetables and Sauce' for nutritional accuracy. The alias 'A&W Udon' is retained to preserve retrieval linkage to the original GrabFood listing. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked udon noodle data (FDC ID 2103689) combined with typical Japanese stir-fry sauce and vegetable additions; no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. GrabFood is not used as a nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central is the approved primary source. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite stir-fried udon dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-17 for this specific preparation. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain boiled udon noodles; per standard GI classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 is classified as low. The stir-fried preparation may modestly lower effective GI further. 5. The dish contains fish-derived dashi and is therefore not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Halal status is unconfirmed. 6. Bell peppers are nightshade vegetables; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor personal tolerance.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English food name corrected from ambiguous 'A&W' to 'Udon Noodles with Stir-Fried Vegetables and Sauce'. Japanese local name provided (うどん炒め). Romanized form 'Udon Itame' and common alias 'Yaki Udon' included. G6 satisfied for Japanese cuisine (Japanese native-script name present). Singaporean local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) added as the dish is served in Singapore.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros present: energy_kcal 137.5, protein_g 6.25, fat_g 4.5, carbohydrate_g 17.5. Source is USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 2103689) — an approved primary source. GrabFood is explicitly excluded as a nutrient source. Estimation basis fully documented in nutrient_source and notes. No core macro is zero.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 1.25, sodium_mg 300.0, saturated_fat_g 1.0, sugar_g 2.0 — all present as numeric values, plausible and consistent with the estimated composition.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 55, gi_category 'low' (corrected from 'medium' — GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category per standard classification: low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), glycemic_load_per_serving 38.5, gi_source from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled udon noodles. Analogous estimate basis documented. GI category now consistent with GI value.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided for udon noodle dish (closest_parent match). FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest_parent noodle dish anchor. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented; all match types documented honestly.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance (Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular), and key nutrient profile. Common user queries and ingredient synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate, sodium-driven), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate, high GL per serving), Cardiovascular Disease (low, low saturated/trans fat). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Human condition name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly; machine key is type2_diabetes.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains 15 entries covering English variants, Japanese script forms (焼きうどん, うどん炒め), romanized forms (Udon Itame, Yaki Udon), GrabFood listing alias (A&W Udon), and Singaporean local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). aliases_by_language populated for english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, japanese, and romanized. All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array. Consistency check passed.

Iteration 3: Updated notes to clarify USDA FoodData Central is the approved source and GrabFood is excluded as a nutrient source.
